pathophysiology_summary: Alpha-1 adrenergic blockade (tamsulosin, recently up-titrated for benign prostatic hyperplasia) impairs the compensatory vasoconstriction that normally maintains blood pressure on standing. On assuming the upright posture, gravitational pooling of venous blood in the lower limbs and splanchnic bed reduces venous return and cardiac output; without adequate reflex arteriolar vasoconstriction, cerebral perfusion falls, producing presyncopal lightheadedness, transient visual dimming and the characteristic sub-occipital/shoulder 'coat-hanger' ache from ischaemia of postural neck muscles. Symptoms resolve within seconds of recumbency as venous return and cerebral perfusion are restored. The measured sustained fall in systolic and diastolic pressure on active stand testing (>30/15 mmHg) confirms orthostatic hypotension; the absence of compensatory tachycardia commensurate with the pressure drop and the temporal link to the alpha-blocker up-titration point to a medication-induced (iatrogenic) mechanism rather than a primary cardiac arrhythmia or a reflex/vasovagal event.

differential.evidence_basis: Symptoms strictly reproducible on standing and relieved within seconds by sitting/lying — the hallmark postural pattern.
Presyncope/near-collapse in an elderly patient must not be attributed to a benign cause until an arrhythmic/ischaemic mechanism is excluded — cannot be excluded via telehealth.
Can cause presyncope, but is typically situational and not strictly reproduced by every postural change.
Positional symptoms — but the complaint is blacking-out/presyncope, not true rotatory vertigo.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the postural trigger points strongly to orthostatic hypotension, but the important-not-to-miss cardiac cause must be actively screened for and excluded in person.

differential.evidence_basis: Recent alpha-blocker dose increase supplies a clear precipitant; symptoms only on standing, never while seated, and relieved by recumbency.
No palpitations and no symptoms at rest lower the probability, but ECG/rhythm assessment is still required to exclude it and cannot be done remotely.
Patient describes blacking-out, not the room spinning — not consistent with true vertigo.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The recent alpha-blocker up-titration is the pivotal elicited clue; recognising it distinguishes an iatrogenic cause from an idiopathic one.

differential.evidence_basis: Active-stand testing shows a sustained >30/15 mmHg fall in blood pressure without proportionate compensatory tachycardia; ECG excludes an acute arrhythmia/ischaemia.
Regular rhythm on examination and no ECG abnormality — arrhythmic syncope excluded once assessed in person.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Confirmation requires in-person orthostatic vitals and an ECG — investigations the AI Doctor cannot perform, hence urgent in-person assessment rather than a remote diagnosis.

differential.evidence_basis: Reproducible postural presyncope, relief on recumbency, coat-hanger ache, recent tamsulosin up-titration, confirmed orthostatic BP drop, and exclusion of a cardiac cause on in-person assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A benign, treatable, self-limiting diagnosis once the offending drug is withheld — but only safe to land after a cardiac cause is excluded in person.

red_flags.description: Recurrent postural presyncope with a near-collapse in an elderly patient living alone — high risk of an injurious fall
red_flags.clinical_significance: Recurrent orthostatic falls in an older adult without anyone at home carry a substantial risk of fracture, head injury and a long-lie; mandates urgent risk reduction and assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A subsequent unwitnessed fall could cause a fracture or head injury with prolonged time on the floor before help arrives.

red_flags.description: Possible cardiac (arrhythmic/ischaemic) cause of presyncope not excludable via telehealth
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presyncope in an elderly patient can be the herald of a dangerous arrhythmia or ischaemia; only an in-person examination and ECG can safely exclude it, so a remote benign label is unsafe.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on orthostatic hypotension and reassuring at home could miss an intermittent arrhythmia that later causes true syncope or sudden death.

red_flags.description: Recent alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) dose increase as an iatrogenic precipitant
red_flags.clinical_significance: The offending drug should be withheld and reviewed; continuing it perpetuates the hypotension and the fall risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to recognise the drug link leaves the cause untreated and the patient exposed to further falls.

red_flags.description: No chest pain, breathlessness, palpitations, loss of consciousness or head strike at present
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence keeps the presentation out of the immediate 000 tier, but each must be safety-netted as an escalation trigger because any could emerge.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If these negatives are assumed rather than checked, an evolving cardiac event could be under-triaged.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling this orthostatic hypotension remotely and reassuring the patient without arranging the in-person orthostatic vitals and ECG needed to exclude a cardiac cause.
Mistaking postural presyncope for BPPV and offering vestibular advice or an antiemetic, when the patient describes blacking-out rather than spinning.
Failing to ask about recent medication changes and so missing the recent alpha-blocker up-titration that is driving the hypotension.
Reflexively dispatching a 000 ambulance for a patient who has recovered and is haemodynamically stable, rather than arranging an urgent same-day in-person assessment.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Sustained fall of >30/15 mmHg on standing (supine 135/80 to standing 105/65 at 3 minutes) without proportionate compensatory tachycardia
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Confirms orthostatic hypotension; the modest HR rise (72 to 88) argues against an appropriate baroreflex response and is consistent with an alpha-blocker effect.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Supine and standing blood pressure and heart rate (active-stand orthostatic vitals)
Cardiac auscultation for murmurs and rhythm regularity
12-lead ECG to exclude arrhythmia or ischaemia
Focal neurological examination and assessment for occult head/limb injury from the near-fall
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange an URGENT same-day in-person assessment (home visit, urgent care, or same-day GP) to perform orthostatic vitals and an ECG, exclude a cardiac cause, and check for injury; advise withholding the next dose of the prostate medication pending that review, plus interim fall-prevention and counter-manoeuvre advice. Escalate to 000 only if chest pain, breathlessness, true loss of consciousness, a head strike, or an injurious fall occurs.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the postural pattern is classic, but confidently calling it benign remotely risks missing an arrhythmic cause that only in-person assessment and an ECG can exclude.

guidelines_referenced.reference: AHA/ACC/HRS 2017 Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Syncope
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Orthostatic vitals and a 12-lead ECG are part of the initial evaluation of syncope/presyncope; medication review is essential where drug-induced orthostatic hypotension is suspected.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Consensus statement on the definition of orthostatic hypotension (2011)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A sustained fall of >=20 mmHg systolic or >=10 mmHg diastolic within 3 minutes of standing defines orthostatic hypotension.
